senses.definition: Relating to the belief that the universe has no independent reality, often seeing only God or absolute spirit as truly real.
examples: The philosopher proposed an acosmic view in which the world has no independent existence.
Some religious traditions include strongly acosmic interpretations of reality.

senses.definition: The philosophical belief that the universe or world has no independent reality, and that only God or absolute spirit truly exists.
examples: Acosmism appears in some readings of Spinoza and certain strands of Hindu thought.
Hegel discussed whether Spinoza’s system really leads to acosmism.

senses.definition: Different forms or examples of the belief that the world has no independent reality and that only God or absolute spirit is truly real.
examples: The article compares several modern acosmisms in Continental philosophy.
Scholars have identified various religious acosmisms across cultures.

senses.definition: A person who believes in or teaches the view that the world has no independent reality and that only God or absolute spirit truly exists.
examples: The acosmist argued that the changing world is only an appearance.
As an acosmist, he denied that the universe has reality apart from God.

senses.definition: Having the character of acosmism or related to the belief that the world has no independent reality.
examples: He defended an acosmistic interpretation of the ancient text.
Certain mystical writings have a strongly acosmistic tone.

senses.definition: People who believe in or teach the view that the world has no independent reality and that only God or absolute spirit truly exists.
examples: Some thinkers have been labeled acosmists by their critics.
Modern acosmists often reject traditional religious language.

senses.definition: A rare spelling of "accost": to approach someone and speak to them in a bold, often rude or unwanted way.
examples: He was suddenly acosted by a stranger in the alley.
Tourists reported being acosted by aggressive street sellers.

senses.definition: A plant embryo or seedling that has no cotyledons, that is, no first seed leaves.
examples: The species was described as an acotyledon because its embryo lacks seed leaves.
In botany, an acotyledon contrasts with monocot and dicot seedlings.

senses.definition: Describing a plant embryo or seedling that does not have cotyledons, the first seed leaves.
examples: The plant has an acotyledonous embryo that lacks seed leaves.
Some parasitic flowering plants are acotyledonous at maturity.

senses.definition: Plants or embryos that do not have cotyledons, the first seed leaves.
examples: The group includes several acotyledons that lack visible seed leaves.
Acotyledons are sometimes contrasted with monocots and dicots in textbooks.

senses.definition: A sound that a person hears when there is no actual external noise, often considered a type of auditory hallucination.
examples: The patient described an acouasm, hearing a bell that no one else heard.
Repeated acouasms can be a symptom that doctors take seriously.

senses.definition: A small South American rodent, similar to an agouti, found in tropical forests.
examples: The acouchi quickly disappeared into the undergrowth.
Researchers observed the acouchi feeding on fallen fruit.

senses.definition: More than one acouchi, small South American rodents that live in tropical forests.
examples: Several acouchies were seen near the river bank.
Acouchies often bury seeds and nuts, helping forest regeneration.

senses.definition: An alternative plural form of acouchi, referring to several of these small South American rodents.
examples: The field notes mention acouchis as common seed dispersers.
In this region, acouchis are more active at dusk.

senses.definition: A variant spelling of acouchi, a small rodent from South American tropical forests.
examples: The acouchy is similar in appearance to the agouti.
Locals sometimes hunt the acouchy for food.

senses.definition: A device used to measure a person’s hearing ability or the strength of sound.
examples: The doctor used an acoumeter during the hearing test.
Technicians calibrated the acoumeter before the experiment.

senses.definition: The measurement and study of hearing ability using special tests and instruments.
examples: Acoumetry is an important part of diagnosing hearing loss.
Modern acoumetry often uses electronic equipment and computer analysis.

senses.definition: In a direction opposite to something else; against or in reverse, especially in older or dialect use.
examples: The ship sailed acounter the strong current.
He turned acounter to the direction of the wind.

senses.definition: A variant form of acoumeter, a device used to measure hearing ability or sound strength.
examples: An acouometer was used to check the workers’ hearing each year.
The old acouometer required manual recording of the results.

senses.definition: Abnormal sensitivity to sounds, when ordinary noises seem unpleasantly loud or painful.
examples: People with acouophonia may find everyday traffic noises unbearable.
Her acouophonia made it hard to stay in crowded places.

senses.definition: A rare or dialect word for a sudden blow or stroke, especially of wind or water.
examples: An acoup of wind shook the small boat.
The door slammed shut with an acoup as the storm hit.

senses.definition: A name for certain tropical American fish, especially big sea catfish, used for food.
examples: Fishermen brought several acoupa to the market.
The soup was made from fresh acoupa caught that morning.

senses.definition: A rare variant of acoupa, referring to certain tropical American food fish.
examples: They served grilled acoupe with rice and vegetables.
Local fishers rely on acoupe as a major source of income.

senses.definition: A sound heard without a clear source, often used for simple auditory hallucinations like buzzing or ringing.
examples: The patient described a constant acousma in his left ear.
An acousma such as ringing can be very distracting.

senses.definition: Plural of acousma, referring to several sounds heard without a real external source.
examples: He suffered from frequent acousmas, including buzzing and hissing.
The therapist asked her to describe her acousmas in detail.

senses.definition: Plural of acousma; in some contexts, short unexplained sounds heard without a visible source, or in music, sounds heard without seeing their cause.
examples: The composer filled the piece with strange acousmata coming from hidden speakers.
In psychiatry, acousmata are classified as simple auditory hallucinations.

senses.definition: Describing a sound that is heard without seeing what produces it, especially in certain kinds of experimental music.
examples: The concert featured acousmatic pieces played through surround speakers.
Acousmatic sound design can make a film scene feel mysterious.

senses.definition: Relating to sound or to the way sound behaves in a room or space.
examples: The hall has excellent acoustic properties for concerts.
Engineers studied the acoustic design of the new auditorium.

senses.definition: Using natural sound without electric amplification, especially about musical instruments or performances.
examples: She played an acoustic guitar instead of an electric one.
They recorded an acoustic version of the song in one take.

senses.definition: A musical performance or recording that uses only non‑electric instruments.
examples: They did an acoustic for the live radio show.
Fans loved the stripped‑down acoustic of the hit single.

senses.definition: Relating to the science of sound or to how sound behaves in spaces, often used in technical or formal contexts.
examples: Acoustical engineers worked on reducing noise in the building.
They installed special panels for acoustical treatment of the studio.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to sound or its behavior in a space.
examples: The room is acoustically unsuitable for chamber music.
The two versions are identical musically but very different acoustically.

senses.definition: A person who studies or works with sound and acoustics, for example in designing buildings or audio equipment.
examples: An acoustician was hired to improve the sound in the concert hall.
As an acoustician, she specializes in noise control in offices.

senses.definition: People who study or work with sound and acoustics, often in building design, audio technology, or noise control.
examples: Acousticians were involved at every stage of the theater’s design.
Modern acousticians use computer models to predict how rooms will sound.

senses.definition: Relating to both hearing and the side of the body or a lateral structure, used in older anatomical descriptions.
examples: The term acousticolateral was used in early studies of the inner ear.
Acousticolateral pathways were mapped in the animal’s brainstem.

senses.definition: An early type of hearing aid or sound‑amplifying device worn to help people hear better.
examples: He relied on a small acousticon to follow the conversation.
The museum displays an old acousticon from the 1920s.

senses.definition: An extreme and irrational fear of certain sounds or of noise in general.
examples: Her acousticophobia made it difficult to live in a busy city.
People with acousticophobia may avoid events with loud music.

senses.definition: The way sound behaves in a room or building, especially how well it supports clear hearing.
examples: The acoustics in this hall are perfect for classical music.
Poor acoustics made it hard to understand the speaker.

senses.definition: The scientific study of sound and how it moves through air and other materials.
examples: She is studying acoustics at university.
Acoustics plays an important role in designing quiet cars.

senses.definition: Relating to electric effects that are produced or influenced by sound waves in certain materials.
examples: The acoustoelectric effect is used in some advanced sensors.
Acoustoelectric devices can convert sound energy into electrical signals.

senses.definition: A short form sometimes used for "accepted" in technical or publishing contexts, often in notes about when a paper was accepted.
examples: Received 5 May; acpt 10 July.
The status of the manuscript changed to acpt yesterday.

senses.definition: To make someone know or aware of something.
examples: We should acquaint students with the rules at the start of the course.
The guide will acquaint you with the basic safety procedures.

senses.definition: To become familiar with someone or something.
examples: I need to acquaint myself with the new software.
She took time to acquaint herself with local customs.

senses.definition: A person you know slightly but who is not a close friend.
examples: He is just an acquaintance from work, not a close friend.
We met through a mutual acquaintance at a conference.

senses.definition: The fact of knowing something or someone in a fairly limited way.
examples: She has some acquaintance with Japanese culture.
Our brief acquaintance with the city left us wanting to return.

senses.definition: People you know slightly but who are not close friends.
examples: I met several old acquaintances at the reunion.
Most of my colleagues are acquaintances rather than close friends.

senses.definition: The state of knowing someone slightly rather than closely.
examples: Our acquaintanceship goes back many years, but we have never been close.
A brief acquaintanceship at college later developed into a strong friendship.

senses.definition: Several relationships where people know each other slightly but are not close friends.
examples: Over the years he formed many acquaintanceships through his work.
Her professional life is full of useful acquaintanceships.

senses.definition: The condition of being acquainted with someone or something; acquaintance, especially in older or more formal use.
examples: Their acquaintancy soon grew into deep friendship.
He mentioned his long acquaintancy with the poet.

senses.definition: A person with whom one is acquainted; an acquaintance, used rarely or in older texts.
examples: He greeted each acquaintant with a polite nod.
An old acquaintant wrote to him after many years of silence.

senses.definition: Knowing something or someone in a fairly limited way.
examples: I am well acquainted with her work.
They are not personally acquainted, but they have many friends in common.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of acquaint: made someone know or aware of something.
examples: We acquainted the staff with the new policy.
The manual acquainted users with the basic features.

senses.definition: The quality or state of being acquainted with someone or something.
examples: Her acquaintedness with local customs helped her avoid mistakes.
A certain acquaintedness is needed before you can work independently.

senses.definition: Present participle of acquaint: making someone know or aware of something.
examples: The course is aimed at acquainting students with basic research methods.
We are acquainting new staff with company procedures.

senses.definition: Third person singular of acquaint: makes someone know or aware of something.
examples: The handbook acquaints new members with the club’s rules.
This chapter acquaints the reader with key terms.

senses.definition: An old or dialect form of acquainted, meaning familiar with something or someone.
examples: I am not acquent with his latest work.
She grew acquent with the village over many years.
